text: This video shows our laparoscopic management of a big five centimeter submucous myoma in case of a fertility surgery.
In case of myoma less than seven centimeters we have no other use to do a vascular control of the uterus by occlusion of both uterine artery at their origin.
The surgeon prepares to make an incision using a bipolar instrument, likely targeting the area around the submucous myoma for laparoscopic management.
The surgeon uses scissors to make a longitudinal incision through the myometrium, carefully cutting until the capsule of the submucous myoma is exposed.
Upon locating the myoma, the surgeon applies traction to the myoma to achieve hemostasis, while carefully removing the connective tissue between the myoma and the surrounding healthy myometrium.
The surgeon extends the length of the longitudinal incision on the uterus, preparing to access the submucous myoma for removal.
The surgeon removes the capsule surrounding the myoma, revealing the underlying tissue structure.
The surgeon uses forceps to apply traction and counter-traction, attempting to remove the myoma from its bed within the uterine tissue.
The surgeon identifies a submucous myoma, indicating its location beneath the mucosal layer of the uterus.

The surgeon carefully positions forceps below the submucous myoma, applying traction to extract it while avoiding damage to the underlying endometrium. Energy devices are not used near the endometrium to prevent injury.
The assistant uses forceps to apply traction and counter-traction, attempting to remove the submucous myoma from its bed, while avoiding damage to the underlying endometrium.
The surgeon demonstrates a technique to safely remove the myoma without damaging the intact endometrium, which is visible adjacent to the myoma.
The surgeon demonstrates the removal of a submucous myoma using forceps, ensuring no damage to the endometrial cavity during the extraction process.

The surgeon applies energy directly to the myoma, ensuring that the surrounding healthy myometrium remains unaffected.
The surgeon removes a small bleeder surrounding the myoma, and the myoma is completely extracted from its bed.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for any significant bleeders, identifying a large bleeder near the site of the myoma removal.
The surgeon performs a small coagulation on the surgical site, but primarily achieves hemostasis by placing stitches to control bleeding.
The surgeon uses a zero Vicryl suture with a fourteen millimeter needle to perform stitching in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs interrupted sutures, placing each stitch two and a half centimeters outside the scar, and repeats the same procedure on the opposite side.
have the advantage to invaginate the healthy tissue
The surgeon works inside the myoma bed, applying techniques to decrease the risk of intrauterine hematoma and promote strong cicatrization.
The surgeon places healthy tissue and endometrium inside the myoma bed, visually integrating these structures within the surgical site.

The surgeon uses the intracorporeal knotting technique to lock the knot on the suture, securing the closure of the myoma bed. For the five-centimeter myoma, three or four knots are tied, with the second knot currently being placed.
The surgeon uses the same needle and technique to place sutures one or two centimeters lateral to the edge of the scar, continuing the interrupted suture method.
The surgeon removes the needle and performs the same suturing movement on the opposite side of the myoma bed.
it's important to use as less as possible the bipolar energy to have a healthy tissue for a fast and a good cicatrization.
The surgeon uses intracorporeal knotting technique to invaginate healthy tissue into the myoma bed, ensuring proper placement within the surgical site.
The surgeon rinses the pelvis to remove blood between the two knots, aiming to decrease post-operative adhesion, before proceeding to tie the last knot using intracorporeal knotting technique.
The surgeon performs a superior knot using intracorporeal knotting technique, while rinsing the pelvis to remove blood and decrease the risk of post-operative adhesion between the bowel and uterus.
The surgeon performs the final knot using intracorporeal knotting technique, carefully avoiding placing the stitch into the cornea while closing the knot around the small myoma.
The suture material will be removed after completing the intracorporeal knotting.
The surgeon demonstrates a perfect coaptation between the two edges of the scar, with no visible bleeding from the scar site.
The surgeon positions the morcellator on the left lateral trochlea to begin morcellating the myoma, preparing to fragment and remove the fibroid tissue.
The final view of the surgical field shows the completed myomectomy with no visible bleeding and well-aligned tissue edges.
The surgical field within the pelvis is clear, with no visible blood present.
The surgeon removes debris and cleans the pelvic cavity, ensuring a clear surgical field.
The surgeon places a fascia closer to seal the hole created by the morcellator, which is larger than twelve millimeters, minimizing the risk of complications.
